origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'əba 'dung', Amh. əbəṭ.
ETHIOPIC: Te. 'əbəda 'gain power over somebody'.
from Ar. 'abd.
perhaps a symbolic interpretation of the name Obadia (Getatchew).

meaning: 'make, do'; mä'əbəlal, mä'əbəlt (pl. mä'əbəlal, mä'əbəlt) 'tool, instrument, fittings, arrow, weapon (arrow, spear)'
origin_explanation: is either a metathesis of məb'əl (see b'əl I) or is to be compared with 'ml 'work', with alternance of labials (Brockelmann 1908:226). Ar. 'amila 'work, make', Heb. 'āmal 'labor, toil', Aram. 'əmal 'work, do', Syr. 'əmal, Md. 'mila 'well-worked', Akk. nēmelu 'profit, gain'. Noeldeke 1910:55 derives Ar. mi'bəla 'arrowhead' from G. mə'əbəl, and ib. he separates G. mə'əbəl 'instrument' from mə'əbəl 'arrow'.

meaning: 'flow'; mä'əbəlal, mä'əbəlalāt (pl. mä'əbəlal, mä'əbəlalāt) 'wave, flood, waved molding, (transferred mean-ing) wreath (that is, something with a wavy shape), (T) flash, dazzling light' (is probably Tayyā's interpretation of mä'əbəla zawarq 'a golden wreath' of Ex. 25:11, 24);
origin_explanation: it is possible that K reconstructed the verb 'əbəla 'flow' from the noun mä'əbəl.
For mä'əbäl 'wave, storm', Amh. məbäl, ma'əbäl from Geez. See also m'bəl, below.

meaning: M 404 misread, Tayyā's ሐረፍተ k"ərrəfta 'sulk-ing' and wrongly rendered G. 'əbäl by 'grudge'.

origin_explanation: əbəq 'scabies', itch, sores, skin rash, leprosy'; əbuq 'scabby'; SEMITIC: Ar. 'əbəqiya 'scar on the face', Akk. epqu bəḥəqə (Dillmann 985, with reservations). ETHIOPIC: Te. 'əbəqa 'be scabby', 'əbəq 'scabies', Amh. əbəq 'itchy rash'; also in Cushitic: Khamta 'awq-ō. See bək, below.

meaning: 'dry up, be arid, grow barren (earth), be sterile' əbəra, causative; 'əbər 'arid, dry, sterile (Gr.255) untruthful'; 'dryness, bareness, scarcity, drought, fam-ine, penury, sterility' (M 455 wrongly translat-ed Amh. dərəq given by Tayyā for 'əbər as disturbo intestinale', while here it means 'drought'); 'əbər (Gr.470) 'dryness'; 'əbər (Lt) 'sterility';

origin_explanation: possibly Ar. ğabara 'dust' (Baumgartner 832 under 'ārābā III); Dillmann 832 (following Gesenius) suggests Heb. 'ārābā 'desert' (for ETHIOPIC: Te. 'abara 'become waste', 'abar 'dryness', Tna. 'abārā 'become meager, become dry, 'abar 'drought', Har. 'abər 'famine', Gur. 'abər 'dry season'; also in Cushitic: Som. 'abār 'dry season', Bil. 'abār 'dryness', Sa.Af. 'abər, 'abār 'famine'.
SEMITIC: Heb. 'ibri. ETHIOPIC: Amh. əbrawi 'Hebrew (person)'.
from Gk. hebraisti 'in Hebrew'; also Amh. əbrayəst 'Hebrew language' (from Geez).

meaning: 'exact compulsory service, compel, force, coerce, take by force (Gr.292) requisition, (Lt) ruin, push, place an obstacle', (KG) = Amh. məlläsa 'return should be hägədd mällašä 'return by force' as given in Y;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sar. 'bt 'compulsory service, calamity' (Müller 1983:277), 'exaction, compulsion' (Biella 350). Akk. ebētu 'tie, bind > tighten, put one in straits', Md. 'əbt 'bind, hold fast' (Dietrich Dillmann 988 (also Brockelmann 1928:504, Leslau 1958:37) suggests Sem. 'bd (Heb. 'ābad) 'work'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'əbätä 'hurl oneself upon to pieces', Amh. əbätä 'trouble, upset, compel'.

meaning: 'be great, be large, be important, become famous, be powerful, increase (intr), be promoted, pre-vail, be raised (voice), (Lt) become fat, be inflated, swell'; (zayə'bay) 'great, big, elder, senior'; 'əbəy 'make great, magnify, increase, augment, enlarge, promote, lift (voice), exalt, extol, exaggerate'; ta'əbaya, ta'əbaya (T) ta'əbaya 'be haughty, become proud, be puffed up, boast, be boast-ful, be arrogant, magnify oneself, be exalted, ta'əbaya 'be arrogant'; 'əbəyyəna 'unruly breed conceit, swagger, exalt, raise'; 'əbəyyəna (K*) 'cause to compete'; 'əbəyy 'proud, arrogant, boastful, haughty, in-solent'; ('əbəyyəna 'presumptuous'); 'əbəy 'greatness, size, magnitude, magnificence, majesty, honor, glory'; 'əbəy (fem. 'əbəy); pl. com. 'əbəyyət) 'big, great, large, important, lofty, elder, old, no-table, loud (voice)'; ('əbəyyət 'great things'); ('əbəyyəy (a) kərəstiyan 'adult Christians', opposed to nəs'usa kərəstiyan; see kərəstiyan); (kəhən 'əbəyy 'high priest'); ('əbəyyəna 'highly, greatly'); 'əbəyyətät (Lt) 'insolent' (pl); ta'(ə)bət 'haughtiness, pride, arrogance, inso-lence, conceit, boasting'; ta'əb(b)əyi, part. of ta'əb(b)əya; ta'əb(b)əyo 'pride';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'əbba ('by) 'fill a sack' (Ar. 'ubiyya 'pride, haughtiness' is considered an Ethiopian loan word by Noeldeke 1910:59 following Horovitz), Heb. 'ābā ('by) 'be thick', Aram. 'əbe, Syr. 'əbā 'thicken', Md. 'əba ('by) 'be thick', Ug. gb-n 'full-ness', Akk. ebū 'be thick'. The connection with Ar. (gby), 'əgbā 'dense, tangled (tree), overgrown' sug-gested by Rabin HS:89, no. 5 is unlikely, since these meanings are secondary from an original meaning 'cover, conceal'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'əbəyä 'be big', 'əbəy 'haughty', sän-bat 'əbəy 'Sunday', Amh. t-əbəyä 'be proud', 'əbəy 'Nile' (lit. 'the big one'), Gur. 'əbəy 'Sunday', passed into Cushitic: Qab.Al.Kam. 'əbə-ta 'Sunday', Bil. 'əbəy 'become big'.
the quoted sentence yanaggəgə 'əgzi'abəher lə'əläma 'əläm wa'add 'God reigns forever' clearly indicates that (wa)'add transcribes Hebrew wā'ed in the ex-pression lə'ōläm wā'ed 'forever'.

meaning: 'still, more again, once more, moreover, further, furthermore, besides,

phonetics: ad, but also', with a negative '(not) yet'
meaning: ad, but also', with a negative '(not) yet'
origin_explanation: also used with suffix pronouns; e.g. `adi-hu` 'he still'. SEMITIC: In connection with the root `uda` ('wd, below'): Ar. `ada` ('wd) used adverbially 'more' (Barth 1902:34; Landberg 1920-42:2338-9), Sar. `d` 'again' (W. Müller), Hars. `ad` 'still, yet, again', Mh. `ad` < `ad`. Heb.Aram. `od` (see also Jean-Hofijzer 203), Ug. `d` 'still'. ETHIOPIC: Te. `adu` 'yet'. Barth 1913:60 analyzes G. `adi` as coming from `ad-ye`, y being a demonstrative.

SEMITIC: Heb. `edy-ek` in Ps. 103:5 is rendered by Driver (JTS 36[1935],155) 'thy prime' and compared with the Geez root. ETHIOPIC: Te. `ad` 'tribe, family, people', Tna. `addi` 'country, village, people'; also in Cushitic: Som. `ed` 'people'.
from Ar. `ud` 'aloe'; also Syr. `uda`, Tna.`ud`, Amh.`ud`.

meaning: be removed to a distance, be discarded, flee, be exiled, be humiliated, (K) remove
origin_explanation: secondary form of `adawa` 'pass' (Dillmann 1013 following Ludolf). SAR. (h)`dw` 'remove (boundary stones)'.
from Amh. `addala`.

meaning: appoint a time, designate, determine, fix (a time), convoke, invite'
origin_explanation: `a'dama, same`.

meaning: ta'addama` 'agree with one another upon a fixed time or on any other matter'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: The connection with Ar. `iddan` 'time', `iddana` 'time', suggested by Dillmann 1009 following Ludolf 501, is doubtful. The root is perhaps related to Ar. `azama` 'invite (to dinner)' with alternation d : z. ETHIOPIC: Tna. `adme` 'age, period of time', Amh. `adme`. Tna. `addama` 'invite to a banquet', Te. `addama` 'make an appointment', Amh. `adma` 'make an appointment', passed into Cushitic: Qem. `adme` 'time', Kham. `edem` 'invite'.
`ba'ala ma-adot` 'Monday after Easter', `ma'(a)daw` (pl. `ma'adaw`) 'ford, passage, ing, anything that makes cross (e.g. `masta'(a)daw` (K*), same`.

meaning: cross over, pass over, pass, transgress, exceed, surpass'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. `ada` '(dy) 'run, pass', Sar. `dw` 'move, march', Soq. `dy` 'cross', Heb. `ada` '(dy) 'pass, pass over', also Wagner 91, Aram. `ada` 'pass by', 'pass near', Md. `ada`, `da` 'pass by, pass over', `dy` (M.C. Astour, JNES 27[1968],25). ETHIOPIC: Te. `ada` '(dy) 'cross', Tna. `(ta)adda`, Amh. `ada`, Har. `ada`, Gur. `ade` passed into Sa. `adaw` 'go'. For `ma'adot`, cp. Te. `ma'mado`, Har. `mādu` 'across'. See also `adaw`.

meaning: ta'adwa` 'go beyond, over, pass by, surpass, exceed, gress, deviate, rebel'
something that one who crosses over'
place where two ways crossroad, fork in the road'
act of crossing, pass over, transgression, violation (of duty)'
the opposite side, passage, bank (of river, sea), ford, ferry boat'
across, to the opposite side, beyond, on the other side of, along)'

origin_explanation: from Ar. `aduww` 'enemy', also in Te. `ado`; it be derived from `adawa` 'transgress, be an enemy'?

SEMITIC: Ar. `ada` '(dy) 'pay, repay' (Dillmann 1011), Akk. `idu` 'hire, wages, rent' (D. Cohen 1979:8). ETHIOPIC: Tna. `ada` 'debt', `adaya` 'pay debt', Amh. `adaya` 'owe (denominative of 'debt'), also in Cushitic: Qua. `ada` 'debt', Qem. `adi`, Kham. `edi`. Cerulli 1951:404 also compares Kafa `era` 'lend'.

meaning: debt, compensation, responsibility, sin, guilt, punishment'
he is held responsible, he is responsible'
origin_explanation: see `dalawa` II).

meaning: tree, shrub, bush, wood, stick, staff (of spear)'
origin_explanation: `adaw` 'herbs, beams forming the frame of a building'.

`ta'adawa` (Sw.in D 1026) 'become a tree'. SEMITIC: Ar. `idah`, any great trees having thorns, (Dat) `ada`, `udah` 'tree', Sar. `d` 'woodwork', Heb. `es` 'tree, wood', Aram. `r` (< `dr`), Eg.Aram. `q`. Ug.Pun. `q`. Akk. `isu`. See also Noeldeke 1910:144-5. ETHIOPIC: Tna. `ansayti`, `ansayti`, `asay` 'tree, wood', Te. `asät`, Amh. `asät`, `as`, `as`, Arg. `ansed`, Gaf `ansa`, Har. `insi`, Gur. `asa`. For `ma'adot`, cp. Te. `ma'mado`, Har. `mādu` 'across'. See also `adaw`.
secondary form of `ada`, below.

`ta'adawa`, (T) `ta'adawa` 'cast lots, be assigned by lot' (denominative); `asta'adawa` 'draw lots, cast lots, cause to draw lots'; `addawa`, `udawa` 'casting lots' (n). SEMITIC: Ar. `ida`(t) 'piece, portion', related to Ar. `hazz` 'fortune, luck, good fortune', Heb. `hes` 'arrow', Ug. `hz` 'lucky'. On a possible relation between 'arrow' (Heb. `hes`, Ar. `hazwa`) and 'fortune', see Hommel 1879:65; Noeldeke 1910:147-8. Fleisch 231 connects 'lot' with `ad` 'bois, morceau de bois pour tirer au sort'. ETHIOPIC: Te. `as` 'lot', Tna. `asa`, `asa`, Amh.Arg. `ata`, Har. `hata`, Hita, Gur. `at`; also in Cushitic: Or. `ita`, Sid. `hita`.

`adaya` (ya'rdi), (T) `asaya` 'breed worms, produce worms, get worms, be eaten by worms, rot, putrefy' (denominative). SEMITIC: related to Ar. `utta` 'moth-worm' (Dillmann 1026); if so, cp Heb. `as`, Aram. `asa`, Syr. `assa`, Akk. `asasu`. ETHIOPIC: Tna. `ase` 'larva', Amh. `as`. Conti Rossini 1912:208 compares Cushitic: Qem. `hasa` 'worm', Qua. `hasi`, Kham. `hesa`, Bil. `gesa`.

meaning: piece of cloth which is as wide as the width of the loom'
origin_explanation: Amh. `farg` 'piece of cloth which is as wide as the width of the loom'.

meaning: OØR, (T) adada HØR, (K) adada OØR 'reap, mow'; (zəyə³dədəd 'mower'); (zəyə³dədəd pl. adadid; for ³adadid; also ³adadidəyən) 'reaper, mower'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'adada 'cut trees, mow', related to haṣada (for which see Jeffery 109), Heb. ma³ăšad 'billhook' (for the bibliography on the various meanings of ³ṣd in the Gezer calendar, see Baumgartner 819), Aram. haṣad 'mow, cut', Syr. 'ad (< ³d < ³dd), Md. hṣd, Ug. m³dd 'harvesting instrument', Akk. eṣēdu 'harvest'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ³aṣäda 'reap', Te. ³aṣda, Haṣa, Amh. aččäda, Arg. haččäda, Gaf. aṣṣäda, Gur. aṣṣäda where the sequence d-d of Proto-Ethiopic became d-d by assimilation, as against R. Hetzron and Habte-Mariam Marcos, JES 4 (1966), 19, n. 1 who explain it by the phoneme d of Geez represented as d in Gurage. For mä³däd 'sickle', cp. Tna. maṣäd, Te. mä³aṣäd, Amh. maččäd, Gaf. maččäda, Gur. mäččäd. The root probably passed into Cushitic: Aw. aṣäd 'mow', Qua. aṣed.

meaning: 'deprive, cause harm, (Lt) affront, do wrong, (Y) rob, take away by force, (K*) become a desert'; ³add, ³add 'desolation, waste, bareness, wilderness, desert'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. ġadda 'lessen, diminish', 'aṣṣa 'become hard' (Dillmann 1027), roots that could explain the Geez meanings 'desolation, bareness'. For the meanings 'rob, take away by force', cp. Ar. 'adda 'seize with the teeth, bite', 'idd 'wicked'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. aṭ(ṭ) 'wilderness'.
SEMITIC: W. Müller suggests, with reservations, Šḥ. 'adl 'old cloth'.
SEMITIC: Ar. 'aẓm 'bone', Heb. 'eṣem, Aram. 'iṭmā, 'aṭmā 'thigh', Ug. 'ẓm 'bone', Ph. 'ṣm, Akk. aṣm. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'aṣäm, 'ačäm 'bone', 'aṣm'a, Arg. haṭam, Amh. aṭäm, aṭäm, Har. aṭ (with m > zero).
could it be a misprint for badawa NØM, below?

meaning: fr, (T.M) ³afara (with >) H&L 'smell nice, give off a good odor'; (³afrat (pl. ³afratät) 'ointment, unguent, fragrant perfume', 'oil, perfume'); (laṣṣäna ³afrat 'sweet tongue' [lit. 'tongue of perfume'], epithet of Apostle Paul').
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'arifa 'use perfumes' (with metathesis), 'arf 'good smell'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. afär 'perfume'; Kham. afir 'hair-oil made of butter'.

meaning: 'cover, put on something'; mä³əfərt, mä³färit 'headband, turban, tiara, (T) short-sleeved shirt'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ġafara 'cover', ġufra 'hair-covering', Heb. 'āper 'headgear' (considered by Zimmern 36, with reservations, an Akkadian loanword), Syr. 'appar 'veil, cover the face', Akk. epēru 'cover the head'. Noeldeke 1901:40 G. mä³əfərt an Aram.-Syr. loanword; ma³pärä 'cloak with a hood', Syr. ma³perä 'hood, one of the Eucharistic vestments'. Note also PBH. ma³āporet 'cloak, wrap'. See also below.

phonetics: ³age (pl. ³ageyät, ³agäyät) O&L (T) ³age H&L
origin_explanation: Amh. hage 'cotton'. See also hage.

meaning: 'place in a row, heap up, make an enclosure, surround with a wall, fence in'; 'agl 'layer, heap, row, pile, enclosure'; 'aglä (D.T) 'enclosure'; the basic meaning is perhaps 'pile up in a circle', hence 'enclosure, palisade'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: for 'enclosure, round', cp. Ar. 'aġala 'wheel', Soq. ig-'alil 'roll oneself', Heb. 'āgal 'be round', Syr. 'eġal; also Aram.-Syr. 'aġal (by dissimilation) 'roll about'. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'agla 'pile up (in a circle), Amh. aggäla 'build a hedge or palisades'.

meaning: 'give birth to, hatch (hen: eggs)'; for ³agala, denominative from ³əgL; one would expect ³ag(g)ala or ³ag(g)ala.

'revolt, rebel, oppress'; ta³aggala 'oppress, force, mistreat, cheat, do violence, maltreat, defraud, be violent, rob, pervert, cause to suffer, be taken by violence, be robbed'; ³a³gala, ³asta³agala (K*), causative; ta³agala, reciprocal of ta³agala; ³asta³agala, caus. of ta³agala; ³agul, ³agL, pass. part. of ³agala, ta³aggäli, part. of ta³agala; also 'robber, oppressor, deceiver'; masta³agäl, act. part. of ³asta³agala; also 'fraudulent person'; ta³agält 'fraud, violence, deed of violence, deceit, oppression, force, ceaseless greed, covetousness, extortion, robbery'; ta³aggälo (T) 'robbery, oppression, ruin'; ta³aggälo 'ready to fight', (tä)³aläga 'struggle, wrestle, strive'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. ³agül 'ready to fight', (with metathesis) 'wrestle, fight', Amh. (t)aggälä 'struggle, wrestle, strive'.
from Ar. 'aġġala 'hurry, rush, urge'.

meaning: 'bear patiently, be tolerant, forbear, endure, persevere, put up with, restrain oneself (from), practise restraint, abstain, refrain from, (Lt) hold a secret'; ³asta³aggäṣa, causative; also 'exercise patience, refrain, bear patiently'; ³aggus 'patient, slow to anger, tolerant'; with possessive suffix pronouns: '(he, she) being patient' (e.g. ³aggusu 'he being patient'); masta³aggäs 'patient, long-suffering, patiently enduring, temperate, ascetic'; ta³aggäṣt 'patience, patient endurance, perseverance, temperance, continence, tolerance, asceticism'; ta³aggäṣi, participle of ta³aggäṣa; also 'patient'; ta³aggäṣo (T) 'endurance, abstinence, self-restraint'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 1014 compares Ar. 'aġaṣa 'withhold, hold back'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ta³aggäṣa 'be patient', Amh.Gur. (t)aggäṣä; passed into Cushitic: Aw. tages-g"a.

phonetics: ³agəṣ ³əsträ OØP&C, (Lt) ³əg ³əsträ OØP&C, (T) ³agustär HØP&C
origin_explanation: perhaps from Gk. hygiesteron 'better for the health' (W. Müller). See also ³agutsär.

meaning: 'encompass, encircle, surround, choke, contain, besiege, lie in ambush'; ³a³gata, causative; ta³agta, pass. of ³agata; ta³agata 'encircle in masses'; ³agut, pass. part. of ³agata; ³agtat 'siege, fortification, scheme, encircling, compassing'; mä³əgat 'confinement, border, snare, net, ambush, fortification', (T) = Amh. askäla 'creeper which is used for making snares or traps for animals'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. ³agta 'fence in, guard', Tna. ³aggätä 'restrain, bridle a horse', Amh. aggätä 'hold back an enemy's assault'.

phonetics: akaka (yə'kak) ዐከከ (K*) 'be full of, contain a lot of water, be full of liquid';
meaning: mə'akak (pl. mə'akakət, mə'akakät) 'basin, tub for bathing, laver, bowl, chamber pot';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Noeldeke 1910:41 compares it with Ar. makkiūk 'measure for grain, drinking cup' and con-siders it borrowed from Aram-Syr. maqiūq 'cup'. See also Fraenkel 207-8. ETHIOPIC: perhaps Te. 'akkät (pl. 'akäk) 'leather vessel for butter'. Tna. 'akkot, from Arabic 'akka (Littmann-Höfner 477).

phonetics: akot ዐኮት, akot ዐኮት 'small locust, dog-fly, (T.M) wasp';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'akkot 'wasp-like insect', perhaps from Cushitic: Kham. agid 'small locust'.

meaning: mə'akät (T.Sw. in D 1394), mə'akətä (D 1401), mə'akəto (T), mə'akəto (OT), maqätä (L), maqätä (L) 'food';
origin_explanation: Dillmann 1401 (following Ludolf 77) considers mə'akətä a corruption of Heb. makkolet (1 Kings 5:11). It is strange that 'anata ዐከተ has the same meaning, and one wonders whether one verb is not a mistake (or misprint?) for the other.
seems to be a reconstructed verb from tä'akäwä 'pal-ace'.
ETHIOPIC: Te. Tna. 'ela 'cistern', Amh. ela 'cistern, well' (from Geez), Har. ela 'stagnant water'; also in Cushitic: Sa.Af.Bil. 'ela, Som. 'el, as against Leslau 1963:24.
a reduplicated root < 'ly, for which see la'ala.

phonetics: alabä ዐለበ (M), 'alabä ዐለበ (T) 'linen, cloth, spinning thread';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'aläbä 'cloth, cotton'; also in Cushit-ic: Qem. alua, Khamta aläbä.
from Ar. 'ulba(t); also Tna. 'albät 'round vessel used for carrying milk', Syr. 'ulba 'sack'; Cushitic: Be. elba 'small box'.
'algat 'genitals, trophy of a battle (e.g. geni-talia), (K.Lt) triumph'; SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. 'alaga 'strive, prevail over-come'. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'allaga 'kill in combat'.

phonetics: allala I ዐለለ (K*) 'separate, excommunicate, (Y) grant someone a fief, de-limit the borders of a fief';
meaning: separate, excommunicate, (Y) grant someone a fief, de-limit the borders of a fief
origin_explanation: (the only example ya'allalu given by Dillmann could mean 'they dye, they sprinkle', and would then belong to 'allala II) 'aläla 'separate, consecrate, excommunicate', ta'allala, pass. of 'allala, 'a'alala 'allul 'separated, segregated, excommuni-cated, (Lt) who dwells apart'; mä'allal 'variety of colors' given by Dillmann 951 belongs to 'allala II; mä'allal (TS) 'fief, (Lt) alcove, recess'; mä'allal 'excommunication, anathema (Sw. in D 951) 'fief, (T) recess'; note that KG strangely renders it by Amh. 'a'af g° 'earthen-ware tripod' instead of the expected 'a'af 'fief'. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'allälä 'distinguish, recognize', Amh. 'allälä 'mark out, set aside a place'. Tna. 'alälä 'separate' comes from lyly. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Bil. aläl 'look at, recognize, distinguish', Sa.Af. ilal.

phonetics: allala II ዐለለ (Y.TS.Lt), 'allala ዐለለ (T) 'dye, color cloth or straw (often red)';
meaning: dye, color cloth or straw (often red)
origin_explanation: 'allul 'dyed, multicolored'; 'alälä (Lt) 'dyed (red)'; mä'allal 'variety of colors'; SEMITIC: Ug. gll 'dip in' (for which see Aistleitner, no. 2147); if so, cp. also Ar. galla 'insert', Heb. 'olel.

phonetics: im, ta'alma ዐልመ (K*) 'be hidden, disap-pear from sight';
origin_explanation: 'alum (K) 'hidden'; this form is given by K as a mistaken correction of 'alamana of Ps. 89:9 (Hebrew text 90:8) to 'alumna; the Septuagint renders it 'our age', from 'alam. SEMITIC: Heb. 'lm (nif'al) 'be hidden', Ug. 'lm 'concealment' (de Moor 172). For the op-meaning of Ar. 'alima 'know' (also Ug.Geez Kopf 161-2, Barr 159. The Geez root is possibly a transcription of Heb. 'lm 'be hidden'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. allämä, allammä alä 'disappear from sight', Tna. 'a'läbä 'hide', with alternance of labials.

phonetics: alamä ዐለመ (D), 'alama ዐለመ (T) 'write, seal, teach, signal, make a sign, mark, indicate';
meaning: write, seal, teach, signal, make a sign, mark, indicate
origin_explanation: ta'alamä 'write down, write below'; 'alämä (Lt.K) 'insignia'; ma'allam 'teacher'; From Ar. 'alama 'mark a thing', 'allama 'teach', 'aläma 'mark, sign', mu'allim 'teacher'; also 'allama 'teach', 'aläma 'make a sign', Tna. 'a'läma 'teach', Amh. allämä 'signal' (v), Te. alam 'banner', Tna. 'alama 'insignia', Amh. (sändäq) alama 'flag, signal'.

phonetics: alam (pl. 'alamät) ዐለም 'world, this world, the secular world, universe, mankind, eternity, lifetime, time';
meaning: world, this world, the secular world, universe, mankind, eternity, lifetime, time
origin_explanation: alam following a noun in the construct state means 'secular', e.g. mak°anna 'alam 'secular judge'; (la'alam 'in eternity, forever'); (la'alam 'forever, from eternity to eterni-ty'); (wǝkä 'alam 'forever'); 'alamäwi 'everlasting, perpetual, eternal'; 'alamäwi 'worldly, of this world, secular, lay-man'; (zalä'alamäwi 'eternal');

'al(l)ama (H) 'create the world' (denomina-tive; cp. also Amh. allämä); SEMITIC: Ar. 'alam 'world' (for its Aram.-Syr. origin, see Jeffery 208), Heb. 'olam, Aram. 'alam, Syr. 'almä, Ug. 'lm 'eternity, universe'. Noeldeke 1910:37-8 considers G. 'alam to be an Aram.-Syr. loanword. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. 'aläm 'world', Amh.Gor. aläm.

SEMITIC: Ar. 'alaqa 'leech', Heb. 'alūqā, Aram. 'alaqtā, Syr. 'elaqtā, Akk. ilqu. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'allägä 'kill in combat', 'alaqit, Arg. halaqit, Gur. alaqat, Har. 'eqti (with l > zero); also in Cushitic: Bil.Qem. inka, inqä (with alternance of liquids).
SEMITIC: Ar. 'alas 'spelt'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'aläs 'oats, rye', Har. 'es, from Or. 'es, is to be identified with 'ls, with loss of the liquid l. The root probably passed into Cushitic: Kam. Qab. alasu.

phonetics: alawa, 'alwa (yə'lu) ዐለወ (D) ዐለወ (D.Y), 'ala-wa, 'alwa ዐለወ (T) 'deal treach-erously, conspire, distort, pervert, act perverse-ly, corrupt, violate (the law), rebel, be rebel-lious, transgress (laws), depart, desert, reject, be faithless, be an apostate, be heretical, change, change in shape, transform, reflect, reverberate, (T.Y.M) turn upside down, trans-late, interpret';
meaning: deal treacherously, conspire, distort, pervert, act perversely, corrupt, violate (the law), rebel, be rebellious, transgress (laws), depart, desert, reject, be faithless, be an apostate, be heretical, change, change in shape, transform, reflect, reverberate, (T.Y.M) turn upside down, translate, interpret
origin_explanation: a'alawa, causative; also 'pervert, corrupt, dis-tort, deviate, translate, copy, transcribe, com-pile'; ta'alwa, passive of 'alawa; 'aläwa (Gr) 'corrupt'; 'aläwi (pl. 'alawt) act. part. of 'alawa; also 'rebel, rebellious, disobedient, wicked, subvert-ed, lawless, heretical, apostate';

meaning: crooked, perverse, evil, perfidious, iniquitous, disobedient, rebellious, rebel, apostate, heretic, heretical; copy'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. `alwā` 'disobedience' (with Koehler 707, and Rabin, Sh 8[1961]396, it could, however, be a metathesis of `awlā`). Syr. `elyā` 'malice, wrong' (see `alava`, below). Rabin is, ib., also compares Ug. `glyt` in Keret II VI.32,45, and Sar. `glyt` 'unjust dealings, wrong'. Sab. Dic. 53 translates Sar. `glyt` 'wrath, overbearingness' and derives it from `gll`; Biella 395 renders it 'hatred'. Geez `alawa` is not to be connected with Sem. `ly` 'be high' contrary to Aistleitner, no. 2030. Margoliouth in JRAS 1939, p.60, derives Ar. `alaw` 'rebel' of Surah 44:19 from G. `alawa`. For the root `wl`, metathesis in relation to `lw`, cp. Ar. `āla` (`awl`) 'deviate from the right course', Heb. `awwel` 'act wrongfully', Aram. Syr. `awlā` 'unrighteousness', Md. `ula`. ETHIOPIC: Te. (`tā)alla` (`tlw`) 'start a riot', `alāwān` `ñā` 'rebel', Tna. `alāwā` 'rebel'; Amh. `allo` `alā` 'deny', `allonña` 'who denies' is to be identified with Amh. `alu` `alā` 'deny', `aluta` 'negation', and is not to be connected with Te. `alāwānña` 'rebel', as suggested by Littmann-Höfner 453. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Sa.Af. `alaw` 'rebel'. See also `alava`.

meaning: disaster, downfall, perfidy, iniquity, perversity, heresy, apostasy, transgression, violating, revolt, rebellion; copy'

phonetics: ar-lāwi, act. part. of 'alawa; also 'copyist, translator'

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. `ala` (`aly`) 'guard, preserve', Tna. `alāyā` 'guard', Amh. `alawa` 'night watch'.

From Gk. `aloe` through Syr. `elway`, `alway` (of Sans-krit origin), also in Ar. `aluwa`, `alwa`, `luwwa`, Heb. `ahalōt`, Md. `huaya`, Te. `alwe`. For the various forms in Arabic and Syriac, see Noeldeke 1910,43, n.4.

meaning: 'rebel, be refractory, shirk, (T) bend, distort, twist, deny, refuse'
deflection, rebellion, revolt, transgressions, plot, conspiracy, deceit, treason, wickedness'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. `t-ly` 'infringement, tampering'. Syr. `aly` (`tly`) 'act perversely'. It is a secondary form of `alawa`, above. See also `m'ly`, `mam'ela`.

SEMITIC: Ar. `am` 'year', `wm` 'year' (?), Sh `on`, `am` (Bittner 1916:39, 51). ETHIOPIC: Te. Tna. `amāt` 'year', Amh. Har.Gur. `amāt`, Arg. `amād`. As for `am`, it occurs in Tna. `ami` 'year', Te. `ta-am` 'last year', Amh. `am` 'year' (from Geez), Amh. Arg. `am-na` 'last year' and the morpheme `-na` for the expression of time in the past). Gur. `am-nā`, `em-na`, Gaf `yaymən`; `allo` `am` 'time', Qem. `amāy` 'year', Amh. `amāti`, Kham. `amerā` (pl. `amāt`), Bil. `amārā` (with alternance `r:t`).

meaning: 'the year of Jesus' incarnation', i.e., His birth)
'the period from the creation of the world to the end of the world')'

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. `um` 'tree', Tna. `om`, Amh. `wo-ma`, `wāma`.
ETHIOPIC: Te. `am'ama` 'spoil' (milk that has stagnated) (Littmann-Höfner 457). See also `amama I`.

meaning: 'erect a column, stand (as a column), (TS) sustain, prop up (with a column), (Y.M.K) strengthen, form (denominative)'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. `imād` 'column' (for which see Jeffery 216). Sar. `md` 'columns', Sh `amud` 'pillar', Amh. Aram. Syr. `ammūdā`, Ph. `md`, Ak. `imdu`. See also Marrassigni 41-2. ETHIOPIC: Tna. `amdi` 'column', Te. `am` `amd` 'column' (from Geez), `ammādā` 'put on umn' passed into Cushitic: Sa. `amid` 'colum'.

meaning: col umn, pillar, post, balustrade, mast, (D 1433,K) column of a page'
'stylite' (solitary ascetic who lives on the top of a high pillar)
'base of a socket, pedestal, (Gr.286) vestibule of a temple or of a palace'
'act unjustly, do violence, commit an iniquity, act wickedly, treat with roughness, harm'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. `gamāsa` 'despise, blame', `magmā` `hi` 'shaken in one's belief', related to Heb. `oppress', Md. `hms` 'suppress, subdue', Ak. `am` 'take off (clothes) by force', `hummusu` 'stro' Brockelmann 1928:241 also compares Aram. `šā` 'shame', Syr. `hammes` 'make ashamed, to share'. Nyberg 192, n.2 connects it with `āmes` 'be strong, prevail', with `-s` becoming in Geez. The derivation of `ammada` from `*am` denominative from `m'd` coming from `d` or `dw` and the comparison with SAr. `m-dw` 'malice', suggested by Praetorius 1890:30-1, is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. `amāsā`, `amṣāṣā` 'do violence, oppress', Te. `amāsa`, Amh. `ammātā`; passed into Cushitic: Bil. `amāṣ`.
SEMITIC: Ar. `gamila` 'be in a corrupt state (plant, or a bandaged wound)'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. `amālā` 'grow moldy', Amh. `am-mālā` 'be spoiled (grain by dampness)'.
SEMITIC: Ar. `gamma` 'cover, conceal', `agamma` 'be overcast (sky)'. This verb is possibly the root of `am'am`, above.
perhaps from Ar. `amma` 'include, comprehend the whole of, be comprehensive'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. `ammāmā` 'be numerous' (from Geez).

SEMITIC: Ar. `amuqa` 'be deep', Soq. `amq` 'middle', Heb. `amaq` 'be deep', Aram. `amaq`, Syr. `əmaq`, Md. `mq`, Ug. `mq` 'valley', Ph. `mq` 'plain', Akk. `emqu` 'wise'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. `amāq` 'a be deep', Te. `amqa` 'be concave, be hollowed', Amh. `amāqā` 'be deep, make deep', `māqmāq` 'abyss' (referring to hell).

meaning: 'make deep, deepen, dig deeply, dig out'
'engage too deeply in' (a form of the reduplicative stem in the spoken languages)
'deep place, the deep, depth, abyss, pit, valley, ravine'

origin_explanation: comes close to samāyāt and one wonders whether it is not a misprint.
SEMITIC: Ar. `aqaba` 'ascent, mountain-road', Heb. `aqob` 'knobby ground', Ug. `qb` 'hilly' (Baumgartner 826).
ETHIOPIC: Tna. `aqab` 'steep hill', Amh. `aqab` 'upward slope, ascent'.
ETHIOPIC: Te. `aqab` 'thorn tree'.

meaning: 'guard, stand guard, watch, keep watch, keep safe, safeguard, keep, tend (flocks), preserve, take care of, care for, take heed, protect, handle with care, observe (the law, a holiday)'; `aqaba` 'put in charge, hand over for safekeeping, keep safe, protect, preserve, give to keep, leave in trust, keep in trust'; `ta`aqaba, `ta`aqaba` 'be watched, be cared for, be kept, be preserved, watch oneself, be watchful, watch out (not to do something), wait, beware of, be on one's guard, take heed, be careful, take care, observe'; (ta`aqaba `amanna 'be preserved from, avoid, stay away from'); `ta`aqaba` 'watch one another'; `asta`aqaba` 'cause to watch, place guards'; `aqqabi` (pl. com. `aqqabi`) 'guardian, guard, keeper, watchman, protector, official'; (`aqqabe` nabih 'orator, spokesman, advocate, observer'); (`aqqabe` r`s, `aqqabe` s`g` 'body guard'); (`aqqabe` sar`t 'Times Keeper' for the emperor's schedules as well as for the hours of the prayers'); (`aqqabe` s`r`y 'physician, magician, witchdoctor' see Worrell I, 182-3); `aqub`, pass. part. of `aqaba`; also 'reserved (for), heedful, cautious'; (`aquba` 'cautiously'); `aqib` 'observance'; `aqib` (fem. of `aqub`, pl. `aqub`t, `aqabt`t) 'concubine' (lit. 'guarded'); `uqabe` 'keeping, observing, watch, custody, protection, safety'; (mal`aka `uqabe` 'guardian angel'); (`saba` `uqabe` 'astrologers'); `uqab`y`wi` 'referring to observing'; `uqab`n` (Gr.470) 'supervision, watch, surveillance'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. `aqab` 'fortress, stronghold, watchtower', m`r`qab `t` 'guards' (W. Müller p.203), Heb. `qb` in ya`qob-el 'God watches you' (Montgomery, JAOS 52[1932].196), Ug. `qb` 'be (from the original meaning 'follow someone closely', see Dietrich-Loretz, BO 23[1966].131). The verb `qb` 'guard' is possibly a denominative from `qb` 'that is, 'be behind someone, watch, guard', `aqab` 'heel', `aqaba` 'come at the heel of', Aram. `qeb` 'heel', Syr. `eqb` 'heel', Heb. `aqeb`, Md. `qba`, Ug. `qbu`.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. `aqqab` 'guard', Te. `aqba`, Amh. `aqqab` 'guard, protect, tend livestock'. Har. `qaba` 'count' (from 'guard, watch > keep track of > count'), Amh. `aqqab` 'cooperative banking in which each member gets his share by drawing lots', lit. 'that which is guarded', Gur. `qab` 'for `aqb` 'concubine', Amh. `aqqab`, `qubat`. The connection between Geez `aqab` and Har. `qba` 'equal (person or one of a pair equal to the other)', ancient Har. `qba` 'fixed' suggested by Cerulli 1936:427 is doubtful. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Som. `ugub` 'guard', Sa.Af. `aqbo` 'protection'. Bilin `akab` 'watch' is borrowed from Tigre. See also `m`qb`.

meaning: 'clay jar placed on top of a hut'.
origin_explanation: probably the same root as `aqd`, below.
`m`r`qad` (pl. `m`r`qad`) architectural term. (T.M)

meaning: 'central part of the frame of the roof, structure of a roof'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. `aqd` structure of a building. The noun `m`r`qad` could be derived from `aqd` 'tie', possibly indicating the beams tied to one another. See also `aqad`, above.

(T) `aqada` `tie, bind', `ta`aqada (K*) passive, `aqd`, `aqd` 'tied, bound', `aqad` (K*) 'binding, tying' (n). SEMITIC: Ar. `aqada` 'tie, bind', Sar. m-`qd` 'fixed, determined' (?), Sh `aqd` 'make a marriage contract', Heb. `aqad` 'tie, bind', Aram. `aqd` 'tie, bind', Md. `aqd`. ETHIOPIC: Te. `aqada` 'knot', Gaf. `aqqada`, `agada`, with `q-d` > `g-d` (so also Cerulli 1936:231, Polotsky 1938:149, n.5); passed into Cushitic: Sa. `akad` 'tie'. See also `aqd`, above.

meaning: 'trip someone, make stumble, hinder, impede, present an obstacle to someone, offend'.
origin_explanation: `aqafa` see `a`qafa`.

meaning: `ta`aqafa, `ta`aqafa` 'trip, stumble, be hindered, be offended, be scandalized, be shocked, be crooked, be perverse'; `ta`aqafa` 'withstand, make one another stumble, (Lt) be on bad terms, think disparage'; `aquf`, pass. part. of `a`qafa`; also 'recipient of offense, offensive, offensive'; `uqaf` 'stumbling block'; `aqfat` (Lt) 'contest, variance'; `aqaft` 'impediment, stumbling block, hindrance, scandal, offense, vexation, fault'; `a`qaf` (Gr), act. part. of `a`qafa`; `m`a`qaf`, `m`a`qaf`, `m`a`qaft` 'stumbling hindrance, offense, cause of offense, insult'; `m`a`qaf`wi` 'scandalous'; `ta`aqaf` 'part. of `a`qafa`; `ta`aqfo` (t) 'scandal'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. `aqaf` 'bend, make hooked'. ETHIOPIC: Te. `anqafa` 'make stumble', Tna. `anqaf` 'cause to stumble'. Tna. `anqaf` 'obstacle', Amh.Arg. `anqafat`, Gaf. `anqafat`, Gur. `anqaf` 'stumble'. Bil. `anqaf` 'stumble'. For the augmented n in the spoken languages, see Praetorius 1879:99; Leslau 1979:LVI.

meaning: 'gather water in a basin or in a reservoir, cause to gather water'.
origin_explanation: (T.Y.Sw. in D 975,M) 'delimit, (K* Lt) gather (tr.,intr), be collected'; `aqala` 'gather water in a basin or in a reservoir, cause to gather water'; `ta`aqala` (Lt), passive; `aql` (Gr) 'lake, pool'; (`aql` b`hr [Gr.469] 'ocean, sea'); `m`q`l` 'pool, pond, cistern, reservoir'; (`m`q`l`t 'reservoir'). SEMITIC: Ar. `aqal` 'collect', `m`qula` 'place that retains rain water'. See also `m`ql`.
From Ar. `aql`; also Te. `aql`, Tna. `aql` 'patience', Amh. `aql` 'intelligence'.

meaning: 'delimit, define, decree, prescribe, encompass, direct, detain, confine, hold in check, restrain'.
origin_explanation: `ta`aqqama`, passive; `aqqam`, act. part. of `aqqama`; `aqqum`, `aqq` 'limited, defined, decreed, determined, who is in full measure, kept within bounds, moderate, (Gr.240) bound, tied'; `aqm` 'measure, limit, enclosure, extent, capacity, moderation, end, completion, maturation, rule, decree'; (`aqma` w`rz`we [Lt] 'adolescence'); (ba-`aqm-a 'moderately, in moderation'); (`aqm-a` + noun 'according to'); `aqqame` 'determination, definition, statute, assigned standing, attendance'; `aqmat` 'fitting proportion'. SEMITIC: Ar. `aqim` 'constricted, obstructed', (Dat) `aqam` 'curb, obstruct', Ar. (Yem) `uqma` 'embankment (of a field)'. ETHIOPIC: Te. `aqqam` 'be able', `aqam` 'ability, ty, strength', Tna. `aqm`, Amh. `aqm` 'ability, limit, confines', Gur. `aqm` 'ability'; passed into Cushitic: Aw. `akam` 'means'.
(B) `anq`n` note that KG I has `q`l` but KG II has `q`n`.

ETHIOPIC: Related to hə'n 'churn butter'. Te. həqan 'buttermilk'. The connection with Amh. anq'alit 'milk curds' (see also G. anq'alit) suggested by Praetorius 1879:VI is unlikely. The root occurs in Cushitic: Sa. 'əqan 'cream'.

meaning: 'drug, medicine, condiment, spice, enchantment by means of drugs'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'aqqar, pl. 'aqaqir 'drug' (of Aramaic origin; see Fraenkel 163), Soq. 'eqre 'shoot, sprout'. Heb. 'iqqar 'root', Aram. 'uqqara, Syr. 'eqqara 'root, medicinal herb'. See for this root Schwally, ZDMG 52 (1898),140-2. ETHIOPIC: Amh. aqaqir 'mixture of spices'.

meaning: 'roll up, wrap up, bind up, knot, confine, retain, collect, (Lt) curdle, form, fashion'; 'aq'ara (DTW 952), causative; ta'aq'ra, passive; also 'be conceived'; (zəviyyəy'aqq'ar 'incomprehensible, inconceivable'); 'aqur, pass. part. of 'aq'ara; 'aq'r (pl. 'aq'ar) 'bundle, sack, bag'; 'aq'rat (Lt) 'bag'; ('aq'rata 'ayn 'pupil of the eye');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the connection with SAr. 'qr 'the wall that supports a reservoir' suggested by Avanzini 1978: 61 is possible. However according to SabDic. 18, SAr. 'qr means perhaps 'land watered by rain'. Brockelmann 1927:28 compares 'qr with Ar. 'aqa- da 'tie, bind', the r of which is due to 'Reimwortbil- dung' with 'asara 'tie, bind'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'aq'ara 'collect, retain water, keep in custody'. Te. 'aqra 'tie up, wrap up'. Amh. 'aqq'ara 'collect'. Har. aqara 'wrap up, make a bundle'; also in Cushitic: Bed. hakur 'tie, bind', Sa. Af. 'aqar. Somali ohol 'leather bag' compared by Reinisch 1902:23 is the Or. okolē and is not to be connected with G. 'aq'ar.

SEMITIC: Ar. 'aqrab 'scorpion', Sh. 'aqreb, Heb. 'aqrab, Aram. 'aqrabba, Syr. 'eqqarbā, Md. 'aqraba (with metathesis), Akk. 'aqrabu (C. R.C. Steiner, AAL 8, 4, p. 18 brings up the possibility of connecting 'qrb 'scorpion' with 'qrb 'mouse' Te. 'arqab, 'arqab (with metathesis), Tna. 'anqərbīt, Amh. 'aqrab. In the meaning 'Scorpio', G. 'aqrab is borrowed from Ar. 'al-'aqrab.

meaning: Amh. 'əmb'ay 'bush having bitter red or yellow fruit'
origin_explanation: see also qarab.

meaning: 'ensnare, entangle, cause to stumble, impede, defraud, (T.M.Gr.291) pierce, afflict'; 'aqqasa 'ensnare, bind the feet, overcome, cast down, repel, suppress, cause to stumble, supplant, defraud, cut off'; ta'aqqasa 'stumble, be ensnared, be thrown down, (T.M) bend'; 'aqqasat, verbal noun of 'aqqasa; 'aqqasi, act. part. of 'aqqasa;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'aqqasa 'twist and plait (the hair)'. Brockelmann 1928:543 compares Syr. 'əqisā 'twisted'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'aqasā, 'anqasā 'make stumble, impede'; passed into Cushitic: Sa. 'aqas 'tie'.
from Heb. 'er through Greek transcription eir.
SEMITIC: Ar. 'arab, 'arabiyy 'Arab', 'arabiyya 'Arabia'. Heb. 'arab 'Arabia'. Syr. 'arabiyā. See also 'arabnat.
transcription of Heb. 'arabā. Note, however, that Bilin also has 'araba 'wilderness, desert' where no transcription of Heb. 'arabā can come into consid- eration.

meaning: 'set (sun, heavenly bodies), (KG) become evening'; 'araba 'cause to set'; 'arab 'west, (Gr.288) sunset'; 'arabi 'western'; 'arbat 'setting (of the sun)'; 'arabawi 'western'; mə'rab 'west, setting (of the sun)'; (mə'raba dahāy 'west'); mə'rabt (KG) 'west, setting (of the sun)'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'araba 'set (sun)', SAr. m-r-b 'west'. Heb. 'arab 'set (sun)', Aram. 'arab, Syr. 'arab, Arb. ph. 'rb, Ug. 'rb, Akk. erēbu. Noeldeke, ZDMG 54 (1900), 155 considers 'rb to be the original Arabic ġ of ġrb being due to the presence of r. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'arābā 'set (sun)'. Amh. 'arab (from Geez). For mə'rab 'west', cp. Te.Tna. Amh. mərab, mə'arab, Gur. marāb, mərāb.

meaning: 'trade, be a merchant'; mə'arab, mə'arabt (OT) mə'arab, mə'arab 'market, trading place, (T) market-goer'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. 'arab 'exchange merchandise', 'articles of exchange'. Fraenkel 1898:74 connects it with Syr. 'arrep 'change money'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. mə'arabt 'market-goers'. I is possible that mə'arab is a transcription of Heb. m'arab and the verb 'arba is a denominative.
possibly from Heb. 'ereb (šabbāt) see Noeldeke 1910:237; also in Tna. 'arbi 'Friday'. Amh. Gaf. arbā; passed into Cushitic: Aw. arbi, Har. harb.

meaning: 'remove the hulls from grain'; 'arabon (Y), pl. 'arabonāt (T) 'hull removed from grain, ground corn, groats'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: 'arbana is perhaps a denominative from 'arabon, from 'rb with the suffix -n and compared with Te. 'arbay 'cutter'.

from Gk. arrhabōn, same of Semitic (Hebrew or Phoenician) origin (Masson 30-31); also in Te. 'ar- bun 'pawn', Tna. 'arbon, Amh. 'arabon. For the Semitic root 'rb, cp. Ar. 'araba 'give earnest-money', SAr. t-r-b 'give pledges', Heb. 'arab 'stand bail for', Aram. 'areb 'vouch for', Syr. 'areb 'pledge oneself, give security', Ug. 'rb 'enter as a pledge', Akk. erubbātu 'pledge'. Driver 1957:64 connects this root with Sem. 'rb 'enter': Akk. erēbu 'enter', Ar. ġaraba 'penetrate (arrow), set (sun, star)' 'for it describes the debtor as "entering" the creditor's house in de- fault of payment'.
from 'arab nat. ETHIOPIC: Amh. arrābā 'make Moroccan leather', from ()'arab 'Arab, foreign' (see Leslau, The Muslim World 39[1949],307-8); Amh. arab also 'red leather'. For nat, see nat.

meaning: 'rest, find rest, come to rest, be at ease, find relief, cease, die, make rest, give rest, give relief'; 'aruf 'resting, still, tranquil, who is at ease, idle, dead'; ('arufa, ba-'aruf 'quietly'); 'arif 'rest' (n); 'urāfe (Gr.469) 'rest'; 'araft 'rest, peace, quiet, respite, repose, comfort, death'; 'arāfi (Lt), act. part. of 'arafa; mə'arāf 'resting place, quiet place, site, resi- dence, support', also 'a measure of length, chapter of a book, section of a book, common antiphonary, collection of hymns and chants, punctuation mark' (see also mə'arāf, below);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the suggestion made by Praetorius 1890:23 to consider G. 'arafa a denominative from Ar. gurfa 'room on the upper floor (where one takes a rest)' is unlikely. Jeffery 65 (following Ludolf) de- rives Ar. 'al-'arāf 'the wall which separates Paradise from Hell' (Surah 7:44) from G. 'arafa 'rest'. Ar. 'arrāf being possibly a corruption of G. mə'arāf. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'arāfa 'take a rest', Tna. 'arāfa, Amh. arrāfa, Arg. harrāfa. As for G. mə'arāf 'a measure of length', and so on, it is also to be derived from 'arafa 'rest, take a rest', the root referring either to a measure of length, that is, a section of the road where one stops or takes a rest, or to a section of a book where one stops or rests before going to the next section. For mə'arāf in this meaning, cp. Ar. 'arf 'mark, sign'.

meaning: 'a measure of length'. Amh. marəf 'antiphonary', see Hammerscmidt 1977:99.

origin_explanation: metathesis in relation to məfərət, for which see ʾəfara.

meaning: 'spoon, handle of a plough, plough (contrary to Brockelmann 1950:17 who sepa- rates ərfa 'spoon' from ərfa 'handle of a plough')'
'spoon serving for administering the Eucharist', lit. 'spoon of the cross' (it is so called because the handle has a little cross)'
'large spoon', SAr. ərəf 'well from which one draws water' (Avanzini 1978:64-6). Mh. ǧərəf 'draw water'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ǧərafa 'scoop', miǧrafa 'big spoon', Dat. maǧrifa 'large spoon', SAr. ərəf 'well from which one draws water' (Avanzini 1978:64-6). Mh. ǧərəf 'draw water'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ərfi 'handle of a plough', Amh. ərfi, Gur. ərəf; also in Cushitic: Bil. ərbəna (pl. ərfan) 'handle of a plough' (passed into Te. ərbaṇa), Som. ərfi. See also Cohen 1947:no. 110. For G. ərfa masqal, cp. Tna. ərfa mäsqal, Amh. ərfa mäsqal (from Geez).

meaning: 'raise, take up, lead up to, offer (sacri- fices, Mass)'
'(ərgata qərbān 'offering of sacrifice, end of sacrifice')'
'ascent, grade, rank, dignity, hierarchy, degree, ladder, step, stair'
'ascend' (for the possibility of its being a Geez loanword, see J. Blau, in Ben- Hayyim 69-72). Heb. ʾərūǧa 'garden bed'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ʾaraǧa 'ascend' (for the possibility of its being a Geez loanword, see J. Blau, in Ben- Hayyim 69-72). Heb. ʾərūǧa 'garden bed'. ETHIOPIC: Te. ərga 'go up', Tna. əraga, Amh.

meaning: 'intercourse' (euphemism 'go up, ascend'; for the euphemism 'have intercourse', from 'go up', cp. Heb. ʿala in Gen. 31:10); passed into Cushitic: Bil əraga 'go up'. For məʾərag 'rank, degree', cp. Tna. maʾərg, Amh. maräg.
origin_explanation: Gur. əraga 'intercourse' (euphemism 'go up, ascend'; for the euphemism 'have intercourse', from 'go up', cp. Heb. ʿala in Gen. 31:10); passed into Cushitic: Bil əraga 'go up'. For məʾərag 'rank, degree', cp. Tna. maʾərg, Amh. maräg.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. erag 'cage full of young birds'.

meaning: (Lt) 'be friendly with'; taʾaraka, (T) taʾaraka 'be friendly with some one, be a friend, make friends, associate, be a companion, be joined'; ʾastaʾaraka 'cause to become friends, recon- cile''

phonetics: ʾark (fem. ʾarakt; pl.m. ʾarkān, fem. ʾarkat, pl. com. ʾarakt)

meaning: 'collection of short hymns or salām at the end of the lives of saints or of the Senkesar (so called after the name of the composer ʾArka Səllus 'a friend of the Trinity')'

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. ʾarki 'friend', taʾaraka 'be friends', Amh. ʾark 'friend'; also in Cushitic: Af. ʾarki 'bride- groom's friend'. In an old Geez graffito, ʾarakt (fem. ʾark) seems to be used as a euphemism for 'snake' (see Littmann in Noeldeke 1910:89). See also ʾarqa II.
From Ar. ʾaraq 'sweat'; also Amh. ʾaraq.
From Ar. ʾaraqi; also Amh. ʾaraqe, ʾaraqi.

meaning: (Lt) 'be naked, be emptied, be empty, be aban- doned, be orphaned, be childless, be bereft (of parents or children), be wanting. (T) be hun- gry''
'make naked, strip naked, strip bare, make empty, empty out, uncover, lay bare, deprive'
'be naked, be strip denuded, be emptied, undress oneself, be be- reft (of parents or children), be void, be de- prived of'
'naked, bare, empty, void, strip, void of, destitute'; also used with possessive suffix pronouns, as in ərūq-u 'he empty- handed'
'emptiness, nakedness'; with possessive suffix pronouns: ('he, she) naked, bare, empty-handed, devoid, alone, merely'
'strip (a bone) of flesh', Heb. ʾaraq 'gnaw', Syr. ʿarreg.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ʾaraqa 'strip (a bone) of flesh', Heb. ʾaraq 'gnaw', Syr. ʿarreg. ETHIOPIC: Te. ʾarqa 'be naked'. Tna. ʾarqa, ʾarāqa, (Tä)raqq 'ätä 'be naked' (denominative from raqut). Gur. əndəraq 'nakedness', where one recognizes ʾaraq preceded by the enigmatic ənd. There is no connection between this root and ʾarqa II 'be friendly', as suggested by Hommel 91, n. 3.

meaning: (Lt) 'be friendly with, be equal, reconcile, effect reconciliation, straighten out''
'make equal, bring about peace, recon- cile'
'be reconciled with one another, make peace with one another'
'mediatorship' (rather than 'naked- ness' as given in Gr.469)
'reconcile'. Te. (attä)ʾarqa 'recon- cile', Gur. (at)arräqa; also in Cushitic: Kham. ərəq, Bil. arak, Sa. ʾaraq, Som. ergo. See also ʾaraka.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. ʾarqa 'reconcile'. Te. (attä)ʾarqa 'recon- cile', Gur. (at)arräqa; also in Cushitic: Kham. ərəq, Bil. arak, Sa. ʾaraq, Som. ergo. See also ʾaraka.
SEMITIC: secondary form of ər, ərra 'be blind', with reduplicated last radical. ETHIOPIC: Amh. ərart 'kind of eye-disease'.

probably for tərrir 'harvest, crop' (from ər), but the meaning 'deserted area' given by Tayyä seems strange. Or would the meaning 'deserted area' de- rive from the meaning 'harvest > harvested > deserted, abandoned'?
ETHIOPIC: Te. Tna. ərər 'lead', Amh. ərər; also in Cushitic: Be. arer 'lead', Kham. arir, Bil. arara 'bullet'.
tərrir (D 1432, 1433) 'equality, the same thing, an equal thing'. ETHIOPIC: Te. (tä)arora 'be equal', a secondary form of ʾarraya, below.
see ərrt I.
probably from Ar. ʾarīš 'hut made of twigs, booth, trellis'; cp. also SAr. ṛṣ, 'shed, hut' (?), Soq. ʾarīš 'hut' (see also Noeldeke 1910:51).

ETHIOPIC: Te. Tna. ərət 'bed'; is not to be connected with Heb. ʿereś 'bed', as suggested by Dillmann 964. The root is probably derived from Cushitic: Bil.kham. Sa.Af. arat, Bil. arät 'rest in the shade (cattle during the heat of the day)'.

meaning: be equal, be even, be leveled off, be level, be smooth, agree with, concur, be the same; make equal, render equal, make straight' (perhaps originally 'a-raya, intransitive; 'a-raya, transitive); 'arraya, (KG) 'a-eraya 'equalize, make equal, make even, make uniform, share equally'; ta-'arraya 'be equal, be the same'; ta-'araya (Lt) 'become equal to one another, be in agreement with one another'; 'asta-'arraya, 'asta-'a-raya 'consider equal, make equal, level'; 'əruy (fem. 'ərit) 'equal, identical, the same, equitable, equivalent'; ('əruya, ba-'əruy 'equally, to the same extent'); 'əräy 'equality'; 'ərrəyat 'equality'; 'ərrəyänna 'equality, state of equality, agree-ment'; 'ərinä 'equality, agreement, (D 1433) the equi-nox'; (ba-'ərinä 'equally'); ta-'ərin 'near'; tərrinä 'equality'; ma-'array 'equal, who makes equal'; 'amä-'raya, see m'ry;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Lindberg 87 compares G. 'arräyä with Ar. 'ayara 'make even'. The connection between this root and G. 'arut 'yoke' from (y)a'raya > 'araya suggested by Praetorius 1890:31 (following Hupfeld) is doubtful. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'əräyä 'reconcile, pacify', ma'əräyä 'be equalized' (denominative from mə'räy). Te. 'ara (y') 'reconcile', Gur. 'ere; also in Cushitic: Qem. 'iru-s 'equalize', 'əräy-s 'make peace', Qua. 'arē 'be equal', Sid. 'arär-si 'reconcile', Som. 'ar 'peace'. See also 'arara.

phonetics: arräzi, act. part. of 'arraza; 'arrəz, (T) 'arrəz 'clothed, dressed'; 'arräz, (T) 'arräz (pl. 'arräzät) 'cloth-ing, garment, vestments, apparel'; ('arräza 'albäs, 'albäsa 'arräz 'changes of rai-ment'); tə'razt 'clothes';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. 'araz 'clothes' (probably from Geez). Har. 'iräz, 'aräz, Gur. 'əraz. This root also came to mean 'be naked', denominative with a privative meaning from 'əraz 'toga, clothes': Amh. 'ərazä 'be naked', Har. 'aräza, Gur. (t) 'aräza. See also 'araza, above.

phonetics: asaba (yə'səb), (KG) 'assaba ONN, (T) 'asaba NNN 'hire for wages, engage, recruit, pay a salary, recompense'; 'assaba 'let (premises), lease'; 'asaba, caus. of 'asaba; ta-'asaba, ta-'asaba, pass. of 'asaba; also 'hire for oneself, lease for oneself'; 'asta-'asaba 'let, lease'; 'assäbi 'hireling, lessor'; 'assäb 'hireling, day laborer, salaried, merce-nary'; 'asb, (Lt) 'äsbo, 'asbo, (T) 'asb 'hire, payment, price, rent, wages, reward, remunera-tion, compensation, profit'; 'assäbe 'remuneration'; 'assäbäwi 'mercenary' (n. adj.); ta-'asäbi, part. of ta-'asba; also 'lessee';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'asaba 'pay hire for a stallion's cover-ing', SAR. s'b 'hiring charge (of beast)'. It is, how-ever, possible that the Ar. and SAR. meaning is derived from the original meaning 'a stallion's cov-ering of a mare' and 'give hire for a stallion's covering' is secondary. ETHIOPIC: Tna. (ta)'asbä 'be hired', 'asbi 'price, salary'. Amh. 'asb 'salary', 'assäbä 'serve for wages' (probably from Geez); passed into Cushitic: Sa. 'asbä 'salary'. See also 'asba diyäqon.

meaning: the central square of the consecrated host put into the chalice';

phonetics: araza OCH (K*) 'be less, be wanting, be de-ficient, be meager'; ta'araza (K*) 'lack clothes or food, be naked'; 'arazännä (K*) 'lack' (n);
origin_explanation: from Amh. 'arräzä 'be wanting'. See also 'arraza.

phonetics: arraza, (K) 'arraza (yə'rəz) OCH 'dress, clothe, furnish clothing'; 'arraza (Lt) 'help to dress'; ta'arraza, (KG) ta'arraza 'be dressed, put on clothes, be clothed';

meaning: transliteration of Chr.-Ar. 'isbädikün, from Gk. so-ma despotikon 'body of the Lord' and resolved by folk etymology into 'asba diyäqon 'salary of the deacon'

phonetics: sb I, ma-'asab ONN 'soothsayer, sorcerer, diviner';
origin_explanation: augmented by n in 'ansaba, above.

SEMITIC: SAR. 'ttr 'star god', Heb. 'aštoret, ancient Syr. 'esterä, Md. 'stira, Ph. 'štrt, Ug. 'ttr(t). Akk. ištar; see G. Garbini, RSO 35 (1960) 25-8; AION 24 (1974).406-10. On the bibliography, see Baumgartner 851. See also 'astaros. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'astär 'heaven', Amh. 'astär (from Geez); passed into Cushitic: Bil. 'astär 'sky'.

phonetics: asaya (yə'si), 'asaya, 'asaya ONP + OWP, (T) 'asaya ONP 'repay, reward, recompense, re-quite (a favor)'; ta'asaya, ta-'asaya, passive; also 'accept pay-ment'; 'assäyi, act. part. of 'asaya;

assuy, pass. part. of 'assaya; 'assət, 'assəyat (pl. 'assəyät), (T) 'assət 'wages, payment, retribution, recompense, remunera-tion, compensation, reward'; ('assəta (T) 'for'; for the development of mean-ing, see bezawa, bezä); tə'sit (K*) 'gift, bribe';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Barth 1893:56 compares Heb. 'äsä ('sy) 'make' with G. 'asaya (so also Dillmann 974) that he translates 'einem antun, entrichten, geben'. He also compares Ar. sa'a ('sy) 'act' (but not so Fraen-kel 1898:32), and SAR. s'y 'sacrifice' (so already Brockelmann 1913:514, n.1, and also W. Müller 1983:277). For the root 'sy ('sy), see also Rundgren 1968:162. ETHIOPIC: Amh. 'asət 'remuneration' (from Geez).
ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. 'asa, Amh.Gur. 'asa, Gaf. 'asä; from Cushitic: Bil. 'asa, Kem.Qua. 'azä.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ak.k. eṣēpu; with metathesis in Ar. dārafa 'redouble', Heb. šāp 'wrap, shawl'. Note Syr. 'eṣap, 'ap 'fold' < 'p < d'p (Brockelmann 1928:517).

phonetics: ta'aṣfa 'wrap oneself, fold', T. 'double', Amh. aṣṣāfa, aṭṭāfā 'bend, fold', Ar. fa. Gur. aṭāfā; also in Cushitic: Bil.Qem. aṭāb 'fold', Qua. ašab, Sa. 'ašab (with s represented s, and b alternating with f). The root is related to 'aṭafa, below.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: T. 'double', Amh. aṣṣāfa, aṭṭāfā 'bend, fold', Ar. fa. Gur. aṭāfā; also in Cushitic: Bil.Qem. aṭāb 'fold', Qua. ašab, Sa. 'ašab (with s represented s, and b alternating with f). The root is related to 'aṭafa, below.

phonetics: aṣq I (pl. 'a'ṣuq, 'a'ṣuqāt, 'a'ṣaqt) ዐጸቀ 'bough, branch, branches (also of candle- tree top, stem (of candlestick), joint, branch, vine branch, cluster (of grapes), es. (Lt) basket (that is, a bundle containing things), cake of figs';

ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'aṣqi 'knot in wood', Amh. aṣq 'joint, articulation', Gaf. aṣqā 'finger', Gur. aṭq 'joint of bamboo, joint of finger'. See also 'aṣq II.

Kutscher, VT 2 [1952],57-69). SAr. t-'sr 'struggle with one another'. As for G. 'aṣir 'juice pressed out' (a passive participle form

phonetics: 'aṣafa (ya'ṣəf) ዐጸፈ (T) 'aṣafa ጸፈ 'bend, fold up, double, put on clothes';
meaning: 'bend, fold up, double, put on clothes'

phonetics: ta'aṣafa 'clothe oneself, cover oneself with a mantle, be clothed, be wrapped';
meaning: 'clothe oneself, cover oneself with a mantle, be clothed, be wrapped'

phonetics: 'aṣf, (T) 'aṣf (pl. 'a'ṣaft, 'aṣfāt) 'cloak, mantle, robe, tunic, raiment, sacerdotal vestment (a cape of leather bound at the waist by a girtle)'; Hammerschmidt 1970:156;
meaning: 'cloak, mantle, robe, tunic, raiment, sacerdotal vestment (a cape of leather bound at the waist by a girtle)'

phonetics: 'uṣāfe 'putting on clothes, manner of putting on clothes';
meaning: 'putting on clothes, manner of putting on clothes'
'act of sealing, act of making the sign of the cross', məʿəṭab (pl. məʿəṭab) 'seal, sign of the cross, cord worn by Christians around the neck, brace-let'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sar. ṭbʿ 'vow, destine' (Müller 1983:278). ETHIOPIC: Tna. ʿəṭaba 'make the sign of the cross', Amh. əṭṭaba 'make the sign of the cross', Amh. əṭṭaba 'cord worn by Christians around the neck'. Te. məʿəṭab, Amh.Gur. məṭab. For the məʿəṭab in the Ethiopian culture, see Walker 18, Taddese Tamrat, BES 9 (1959).38-42. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. məʿəṭab, Bil. məṭab, Or. məṭaba. See also məṭb.

meaning: 'become angry'; əṭṭum (Lt) 'crushed'; maʿəṭṭam, məʿəṭṭam 'angry, prone to anger, irate, passionate, quarrelsome, fierce, furious, irascible'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps Heb. ʿm (nif-al) which occurs only in Is. 9:18 rendered 'verwüstet, erschüttert' (Baumgartner 856). Note that the meaning of Heb. ʿm (nif-al) 'be dark' (so also in various Arabic dialects; see Blau, VT 5(1955),342) may likewise explain the meaning 'get angry' from a metaphorical expression such as 'his face became dark > he became angry'. On the other hand, one wonders whether ʿaṭṭama is not a reconstructed verb from maʿəṭṭəm which could then be a derived nominal form of maʿaṭ 'anger' (see below) with the unusual suffix -m.
SEMITIC: perhaps from Ar.(Aden) ʿaṭar 'pea'. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. ʿaṭar, Amh.Arg.Gur.Har. ʿaṭar, Gaf. ʿaṭarə; in Cushitic: Or.Kam.Tem. ʿaṭara, Qem. Qua. ʿažar, Kham. ʿaḍir, Sa.Af. ʿaṭir.

meaning: 'seize, grasp, lay hands on, hold tightly in one's fist'; əṭṭara 'seize, grasp'. Te. ʿəṭṭara 'seize', Amh. əṭṭarə(t) 'that which can be seized with two fingers'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. ʿəṭara 'seize, grasp'. Te. ʿəṭṭara 'seize', Amh. əṭṭarə(t) 'that which can be seized with two fingers'.

meaning: 'fold, wrap, plait'; təʿəṭafa (K*), passive; ʿəṭaf, pass. part. of ʿəṭafa; also 'which is wo-ven, web, texture'; ʿəṭəf 'which is woven, web, wooden roller around which a weaver winds the finished portion of the cloth'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ʿaṭafa 'fold', Sar. ṭf 'mantle', Šh. ʿoṭaf 'fold', Heb. ʿaṭap 'envelop oneself with', Aram. ʿaṭap, Syr. ʿaṭap 'clothe oneself', Md. ʿaṭap, Ph. ʿp-ṭ 'wrappings' (?). ETHIOPIC: Amh. ʿaṭṭafa 'fold'. See also ʿaṣafa.

meaning: 'burn incense, offer incense, fumigate with in-cense'; ṭaʿəṭna 'be fumigated with incense, (incense)'; ʿaṭani (K*) 'who burns incense'; ʿəṭun 'fumigated with incense'; ʿəṭan (pl. ʿəṭanat) 'incense'; (ṣalota ʿəṭan 'Ritual of the Incense') (ʿəṭana məgar, religious song consisting of eleven verses, sung after the communion); Velat 64; ʿəṭənat 'incense offering'; məʿəṭan 'place where one offers incense'; məʿəṭant (pl. maʿəṭan, maʿəṭant) 'cens-ble, (Lt) fumigation with incense'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to ṭr (with alternance of liquids): ʿaṭṭara 'to perfume', ʿiṭr 'perfume', Šh. ʿaṭr 'perfume', Aram. ʿaṭar 'smoke', Syr. ʿeṭra 'incense'. Heb. ʿaṭar 'perfume' (with r). Brockelmann 1927:28 compares ʿaṭana with Sem. (Ar.Syr) ʿiṭr 'perfume, scent', the n of which is due to 'Reimwortbildung' with tanana 'smoke'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ʿaṭana 'fumigate, perfume, render fragrant with incense'. Te. ʿaṭna, Amh. ʿaṭana, Har. ʿaṭana. For 'incense', cp. Te.Tna. ʿəṭan, Gur. ʿəṭan, tan. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa.Af. ʿəṭan. See also ʿəṭṭar.

meaning: 'gird, gird oneself, gird firmly, put around the waist, fasten, restrain'; ʿaṭaqa, causative; ʿaṭaqi (K*), act. part. of ʿaṭaqa; ʿəṭaq (K*) 'act of girding oneself'; ʿəṭaq (Gr.471) 'girdle, belt'; məʿəṭaqit (Lt) 'waistband'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 1017 analyzes it as ʿaṭaqa and considers it related to Ar. ṭawq 'necklace, collar'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ʿaṭaqa 'gird', Amh. (1) ʿaṭaqa 'gird oneself', Gur. (1) ʿaṭṭaqa 'place a lid over a jar', Te. ʿandaqa 'girdle' (with augmented n and ṭ-q by dissimilation), for which see Litt-mann, ZA 14 (1899).25. A Ambros suggests a connection with G. ṭaʿaqa (with metathesis) 'en-close, clasp'.

SEMITIC: Ar. ʿaṭṭar 'merchant of perfumes, candles, so on' (Dozy), from ʿiṭr 'perfume'. For the root ʿiṭr in the other Semitic languages, see ʿaṭana. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ʿaṭara 'trade', ʿaṭari 'itinerant ped-dler'. Amh. ʿaṭṭara 'sell perfume, be an itinerant peddler'. The root for 'perfume' also occurs in Te. ʿəṭr, Amh. ʿəṭr, Tna. həṭur.
ETHIOPIC: Te. ʿeṭro 'dwarf antelope'.
From Ar. ʿuṭarid.

SEMITIC: Ar. ʿaṭasa 'sneeze', Soq. ʿeṭoš, Heb. ʿaṭaš, Aram. ʿaṭaš, Syr. ʿəṭaša 'sneezing'. ETHIOPIC: Te. ʿaṭaša 'sneeze', Tna. ʿaṭaša, ʿaṭaša, Har. ʿaṭṭəš baya, Gur. ʿaṭṭəša baya, mented by n in Te. hanṭaša, Tna. hanṭaša, ʿəbäla, Amh.Gur. anəṭṭaša. The root probably passed into Cushitic: Bil. heṭiš y, Kam. hanṭəššare, ʿaṭaš, Or. haṭṭiššo.

meaning: 'bending, curvature, broad part of thigh, flesh of thigh, (T.M) front, socket of hip-bone'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ʿaṭā (ʿw) 'bend'.

meaning: 'go around, turn around, circle, encircle, encompass, surround, circulate, revolve, turn'; ʿawwada (T) ʿawwada 'issue a decree, proclaim'; ʿawoda, causative; also 'send around, lead around'; ṭarawwada, pass. of ʿoda; ʿawwadi, act. part. of ʿawwada; ʿawwədi, act. part. of ʿawwada; also 'crier, herald, preacher, messenger'; ʿawwad, ʿawwud, pass. part. of the transitive meanings of ʿoda; ʿawd, (T) ʿawd (pl. ʿawwad, ʿawwədət) 'circle, circuit, cycle, circumference, threshing floor, court, hall, judgment seat, tribunal, court of law, judgment, assembly, council, district, en-virons, neighborhood, vicinity, area, period of time'; ʿawd- (followed by suffix pronouns) 'around, round about'; ʿawda 'around' (adv.); ʿawwadət (Gr.437) 'meeting place of the elders, synagogues'; (ʿawda ʿəkl, ʿawda maʿərar 'threshing floor'); (ʿawda ʿaṣad 'court'); (ʿawda makʿənan 'court of law'); (ʿawda nagaśt 'The Cycle of the Kings', a book dealing with divination and fortune-telling; it consists of circular drawings; see Conti Ros-sini, RSE 1 (1941).127-45; Hammerschmidt-Six 1983:89); (ʿawda qamar 'lunar cycle'); (ʿawda qəṣt 'court of law'); (ʿawda ṣabʿ 'battlefield'); (ʿawda wayn 'vineyard'); (rəʿəsa ʿawda ʿamat 'New Year'); ʿudat, verbal noun of ʿoda; also 'circle, circuit, turn, orbit, revolution, procession'; ʿəwwade (pl. ʿəwwadeyat) 'going around (n.), circling, revolving, circular course, revolution, cycle (of year)'; məwəd 'circle, circuit, periphery, circumfer-ence, border, village'; taʿawwədət 'transgression'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ʿāda (ʿwd) 'return, come back to', Sar. ʿwd 'return', Heb. ʿiwwed 'surround', Ug. ʿw(d) 'go around' (de Moor 130). For the connection with Ar. ʿāda 'custom', Syr. ʿəyada, see Buhl 568, Koehler 685, Baumgartner 751. See also ʿādi, above. ETHIOPIC: Te. (ṭa)ʿawwada 'turn, go around', Amh. ʿawwada 'go around'. The root ʿwd 'go around' is not to be identified with ʿwd 'tell' of the various Ethiopian languages (see Leslau 1979:111-2). For G. ʿawd 'threshing floor', cp. Tna. ʿawd, Te. ʿawd, Gur. ʿawd, Har. ʿud. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Or. ʿuda 'threshing floor', Kam.Qab. ʿoḍu, Sa. ʿaude. The spelling of G. ʿoda is a variant spelling of ʿoda and there is no reason to suggest two different roots ʿoda and ʿoda, as stated, with reservations, by Löfgren 1927:108.

meaning: 'fly'; 'of (pl. əwāf) fowl, bird'; 'of bəhətāwi (Sw. in D 1002) owl'; 'ofa masqal (L) bird that appears around the masqal-festivity'; 'ofa yārəd (L 381) hoopoe';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'āfa ('alā) 'circle over a thing (bird)'. 'awf 'omen, augury' (Noeldeke 1904:85). Soq. 'ef 'fly'. Heb. 'ōf 'bird, fowl'. Aram. 'ūpā, Syr. 'awpā. Ug. 'p-r' 'bird', 'up 'fly'. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'of 'bird', Tna. 'of, 'uf. Har. 'ūf, Gur. 'of. Amh.Arg. wof, Gaf yəf.ā.

meaning: 'be crooked, be curved, be bent, be cross-eyed'; ta'awəga, with (T) = Amh. wāgāššā 'become mad with rage, be furious';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'awiga 'be crooked, be curved'. Pbh. 'āg ('wg) 'draw a circle'. Heb. 'ūgā 'circular loaf of bread' D.H. Müller, WZKM 23 (1909), 353 identifies it with Soq. 'ayeg 'give birth'. Mh. gayg 'man' from the meaning of 'wg. 'eg 'be twisted, be tortuous > have labor pains, give birth', and he compares Heb. hīl 'labor pains' from hwl 'turn to this side and that side'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. awwāgā 'be twisted, be deformed'.

phonetics: wg II, ta.awəga, ta.awwəga ተ+ዐውግ I ተዐውግ, (T) ta.awəga ተ+ዐውግ
meaning: 'deride, mock, jeer, hold in contempt, scorn, make a scornful gesture';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 1001 (so also Rabin, Eretz-Israel 8[1967],253) connects it with wg I stating "ut vide- tur, proprie: oculos, nares distorquere". ETHIOPIC: Amh. (t)awwāgā 'mock, make fun of'.
The verb is reconstructed from the noun 'ugāt which is undoubtedly a transcription of Heb. 'ūgā, a construct state of 'ūgā 'cake'.

meaning: 'deviate, go astray, recoil, swerve, confuse, disturb'; 'awəkkaka (Lt), causative; also 'shake'; 'awkakənnā 'deviation, going astray';
origin_explanation: also written 'awkaka አውከከ which could be inter- preted as an a-stem of wkk. In view of the form 'awkakənnā (or 'awkakənnā), the verb is either a 4- radical wkk or wkkk. Note that M. Höfner in Baumgartner 1967:112 reads 'awkaka 'confusion, dis- tress'. Sar. and S. as for Amh. 'awwāka 'affliction', 'distress'. It connects it with Ar. 'awka 'confusion, dis- tress'. Sar. 'k. As for Amh. 'awwāka 'ib, its origin is hwk, G. hoka, and is not to be connected with wkk.

SEMITIC: possibly related to Syr. 'al'ālā 'whirlwind'. Aram. 'al'ōl (Dillmann 994), Pbh. 'al'ōl, compared by Schulthess 45 with Ar. gāla (gʷl) 'destroy, take unexpectedly'. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'awlay 'wind', Amh. Gur. 'awlo 'whirlwind'; also in Cushitic: Be. 'aūlāy, Kham 'aūlā.

SEMITIC: connected with Ar. 'āla ('wl) 'feed', 'iyāl 'the persons whom one feeds (including chil- dren)'. Šh 'el, Heb. 'ūl, 'ōlel 'suckling'. Aram. 'ūlād 'child', 'ūlā 'foal', Syr. 'ūlā 'newborn baby', 'ūl ('wl) 'child', 'ūl 'nursling', Ug. 'l 'child' (for which see Caquot 264). ETHIOPIC: Te. 'awāl 'young of an elephant', 'əlu 'young of the donkey', Tna. 'illu; also in Cu- shitic: Sa. 'ellu 'foal', Af. allū.
SEMITIC: related to Ar. gamma 'cover, conceal'.

meaning: 'know, understand, take heed (with or without rə's 'head'), take notice (of), beware of, take care of, observe'; impera- tive ('uq) with the negative subjunctive 'take not to..., take heed'; 'awəqa 'make known, show, manifest, make evident, indicate, demonstrate'; ta'awəqa, ta'awwəqa (T) ta'awəqa 'be known (with object suffix pronouns 'be known to', e.g. yət'awwəqo 'it is known to him'), be made known, be revealed, be recognized, be evident, be identified, be noticed, be manifest, be understood'; ta'āwəqa (K*) 'know one another'; 'astə'āwəqa (K*), caus. of ta'āwəqa; 'əwwəq, 'əwwuq 'well-known, familiar, mani- fest, famous'; 'awq 'friend, familiar'; 'uqāt 'knowledge, instruction'; 'awqot 'knowledge';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. awwāgā 'know', Har. 'āqa; Cushitic: Amh. yaq 'know', Som. 'ōq, Qua. ax (Ce- rulli 1936:234).

meaning: 'be blind'; 'awra, a'awra 'blind (a person or an eye), make blind, put out (an eye)'; ta'awwara, (T) ta'awwara 'become blind, show oneself blind to, overlook, treat lightly, disregard, neglect, be negligent, be heedless'; 'əwwər 'blind'; 'əwwār 'blindness'; 'əwwār (pl. 'awāwərt) 'blind'; 'urāt 'blindness'; ta'awwāri, part. of ta'awwara; also 'heed- less'; ta'awwəro, ta'awwəro qāl 'negligence'; t'əwwəwərt (TS.T) 'treating lightly, neglecting, ignoring';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'awar 'blind in one eye, one-eyed'. Soq. 'er 'be blind'. Heb. 'iwwer 'blind'. Ar. 'awira, Syr. 'awira, Md. 'uira, Ug. 'wr. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. 'awər 'blind'. Amh.Arg. 'əwər, Gaf. 'əwwərə, Gur. 'ūr; verbs: Te. 'ora 'be blind', Tna. 'awāra, Amh.Gur. (t)'awwārā. See also 'awrora.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. awtatta 'one who wanders from place to place', wātātā 'wander about from place to place'.
see 'awyawa, below.

meaning: 'wail in mourning, howl, groan, moan, lament, cry out'; 'awəyyawa, causative; 'awyāt 'wailing, lamentation, lamenting, howling, cries of woe';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'awā ('wy) 'shout, howl'. Mh. 'aww, Šh 'ōy 'woe!'. Aram. 'āwē ('wy) 'shout, howl'. Syr. 'awā, Md. 'awa. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'away bālā, 'aw bālā, 'aw 'shout, howl'. Te. wāwā, 'awā 'shout', 'awōy 'lamentation of women at the death of a nobleman', 'away passed into Cushitic: Bil. 'ayg.

(M 457 wrongly translates it 'lip' confusing lābrum 'basin' [given in Dillmann 1008 as la- brum] with lābrum 'lip'). SEMITIC: Ar. 'iggāna 'urn' (Dillmann 1008); possibly Heb. 'aggān 'bowl', Aram.-Syr. 'aggānā, Md. 'agania, Ug. 'agn, Akk. 'agannu (see also D. Cohen 1970:7, Conti 1978:60, n.3). ETHIOPIC: related to G. gan 'big jar', Te. gāna, Tna. gān'i, Amh. gan; also in Cushitic: Som. 'agān. See also Calice, no. 511; Cohen 1947:no. 12.

meaning: 'go astray, wander about restlessly, roam about, ramble, err, turn around'; 'ayəla, causative; also 'lead astray'; ta'ayyala 'roam about, go astray'; 'ayyāl, 'ayālī 'who goes astray, roaming, ram- bling, wandering up and down'; 'ayyud (Lt) 'who goes astray'; 'ilāt 'emigration, the act of roaming, of ram- bling';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. 'āla ('yl) 'lead astray' (Hava). ETHIOPIC: perhaps Te. 'el 'stupid' (that is, someone who roams about aimlessly).

meaning: 'eye, spring, source, tongue (of a balance), engraving (of a seal)'; 'ayn ba-'ayn 'face-to-face'; ('ayna mad 'latrine'); ('ayna warq 'jaundice');

'din', Gaf. yāyā 'shout', Har. 'aw bāya, Amh. 'u-'u 'alā, Gur. 'u balā. Bezold in Dillmann 1907:133, n. 1 reads 'awwāya and derives it from 'awwāya with the causative morpheme -a. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Tem. 'əre, Bil. wāry, Som. 'uwo, Qem. 'aw y. See also 'awya, 'awwəra, and Sasse 42.
'accumulate, gather (intr), col- lect (intr)'; ta'ayəga (Lt) 'form a pond (denominative)'; 'ayg, 'ayg (pl. 'ayāg) 'lake, pond, pool, marsh'; mə'yāg (Gr.470) 'pond';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Soq. 'eẓeḥ 'pond' (Leslau 1938:307); re- lated to Syr. 'igā ('yg) 'stream'. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'ayg 'pond, swamp', Tna. 'ayag; passed into Cushitic: Bil. 'ayg.

phonetics: (la-`ayn `before'); (`za-`ayn, saba `ayn `watchman, supervisor, spy, spies, scout'); (`bənta `ayn `pupil of the eye, eyeball'); (kama qasbata `ayn `in the twinkling of an eye'); `aynat (Lt) `evil eye';
meaning: `ayyəna `contemplate, observe, perceive, view mentally, (T.Y.M) evaluate, examine, inspect, put in order' (denominative); ta`ayyəna `contemplate (observe) attentively, examine, guard, keep'; ta`ay(y)əna `camp, set, set up a camp (possibly `to seek for oneself a well to establish a camp'; so Dillmann 1007), congregate'; with lä`äla, `besiege'; `ayyəyun, pass. part. of `ayyəna; also `considered carefully, selected'; ta`ayyənt (pl. tə`ayyəntat, ta`äyən) `camp, tabernacle, tent, army, hosts, troops, company, assembly, congregation, choir';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. `ayn `eye, source', SAr. `yn, Soq. `ain, Heb. `ayin, Aram. `enä, Syr. `aynä, Md. aina, Ug.Ph. `n `eye', Akk. inu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. `ayni `eye', Te. `in, Amh.Arg.Gur. `ayn, Gaf. inä, Har. in; Tna. `ayyənä `explore the camp of the enemy'. The root is common with Cushitic: Bil. `il `eye', Qua.Kham. el, Af.Som. il.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. `ayyärä `mix, blend'.

phonetics: `ayyəra II, `ayyara OPL `rebuke, reproach, (T) despise, mock, make fun of';
meaning: ta`ayyəra, passive; also `revile, make reproaches, reprove, scoff, insult, affront, offend, despise, defy, (Lt) brag'; `ayyur, pass. participle; ta`ayyərt `reproach, aspersion, offense, disgrace, dishonor, infamy, derision, mockery, insult, abuse, insolence, arrogance, subject of reproach, disgrace'; ta`ayyəro, same;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. `ayyara `revile', SAr. `yr `disgrace, shame', Sh. `er `disgrace', Heb. `yr (`polel `orer') `revile' (Driver, VT 3, Supp. [1955] 72, and Barr 125), Akk. aru (Kinnier Wilson, JSS 7[1962],181).
ETHIOPIC: Te. `ayyärä `insult', Tna. `ayyärä `joke, jest'. Passed into Cushitic: Kham. ier `calumniate, slander', Af. `ayara `give offense'.

meaning: this reading is that of Ludolf, but Dillmann's text has magayyər OPL (as does also the Asmara Bible), for which see gayyara I.

phonetics: `ys, esa OPL (Lt) `soften, grow soft'; ta`esa (D 1397) `be patient';
meaning: see also `ys, esa, even though `ys seems to have an opposite meaning.

phonetics: `ayaya (yə`yay, yə`yəy) OPL (Y.T) `err, wander from, deviate, stray, be of, not understand, be mistaken, be different, be distant, be separated';
meaning: ta`ayaya `be diverted from the right path'; `ayyuy `insane, senseless, dull, stupid, raving, different, distinct, schismatic'; also participial meanings of `ayaya; e.g. `who strays', and so on.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. gawa (gəwy) `err', `awa (wy) `bend, twist', Aram. `awe `curve', `a`awe `be wrong'; von Soden 267 compares Akk. ewû(m), "etwa `belasten mit'".

note that the Amharic translations tätägga and given by T.Y are contradictory since tätägga means `seek protection, lean against', and `astägga `give protection'. Heb. `azab `help' in Jer. 49:25 JQR 28(1937-8):126; see also Barr 140), Akk. alu `help' (n); perhaps SAr. `db `followers, adherents' (W. Müller).
from Amh. `azaba. See also `azaba.

phonetics: `əzl OPL, mode of chant (during important festivities);
meaning: for the other modes, see gə`əz, `arä`ray and Tito Lepisa 163-4; mə`zäl, see below; `asta`azala `sing in the `əzl-mode, sing in response, alternate in reciting the Psalms' (denominative);
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. `əzli, mode of chant, Amh. `əzl. See also `həzl, and Velat 76, 78.
from Ar. `azama; also Amh. `azzämä `practice sorcery'.

phonetics: `azaqa (yə`zaq) OPL (Y.M) `become marshland, (K*) dig, make a hole, drill';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Buhl 578, Baumgartner 766, and 1958:39, following Dillmann 1003, connect Ar. `azaqa `dig up'; cp. also PbH `azaq `dig'. however, the origin of the Geez noun `azaq `well' and verb `azaqa `become muddy', the Arabic comparison would not be valid unless G. `azaqa `become muddy' is a denominative from `azaqät `pit, well'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. `azäqät `deep pit' (from Geez `azäq `swamp', Tna. `azäqti.

meaning: could it be a misprint for `ənq OPL?

ta`azra, ta`azara (L.Gr.296) `make an attack upon, press into, assail, (D) be strewn, be scattered, be diffused, spread itself, (T) vanish from sight', (T.M) ta`azra OPL `go from one place to another' (that is, `scatter oneself', pitch camp (referring to pitching the camp in various places, that is, go from place to place). The passage of Judges 15:9 favors Dillmann's and Tayyä's meanings
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ullendorff 1977:127 accepts Ludolf's translation `make an attack on, assail', and compares Ar. gäzura `be abundant, be copious' (so Dillmann 1003, with reservations), Ug. gzr `hero, noble', and Heb. `zr (nif-al) in 1 Chron. 5:20. For the meanings `be strewn, be scattered', the root `zr could be related to zarzara `scatter', from zr-zr. ETHIOPIC: Tna. `azzärä `bäla, tä`azrä `vanish from sight'.

meaning: mä`azär, mä`azər, mä`azär (pl. ma`azär), (T) mä`azär `shining splendor, brilliant rays, (OT) light, a crown that shines'; mə`zur (Lt) `surrounded with splendor';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. mə`zər `rays of sun'. See also mə`zər.

phonetics: `azaza OPL `be strong, be steadfast, be vigorous, be firm';
meaning: (perhaps `azzaza `be strong', and `azzaza `overwhelm' in `azzazanni `it overwhelmed me' rather than translating `azzaza `become strong' and considering -nni an indirect object, as suggested by Dillmann 1004); ta`azzaza (Lt) `be overwhelmed'; `asta`azzaza `conduct oneself as being strong'; `əzzuz `powerful, mighty, strong, huge'; (`əzzuza `strongly'); `azziz, (T) `azziz (fem. `azzäz) `mighty, strong, vigorous, excellent'; `əzaz `power, strength, vigor, dominion, excellence, dignity';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. `azza `be powerful', SAr. `z-t `strength', Soq. `zz `give strength', Heb. `oz `strength', Aram. `azaz `be strong', Syr. `az `be powerful', Md. azz (`zz) `strengthen', Ph.Ug. `z `strong', Akk. ezēzu `be fierce, be furious'.

phonetics: `zz, `as`azaza OPL (T) `as`azaza OPL `become cramped, grow stiff, be benumbed, be torpid, be stupefied, make benumbed, make stupefied, make obstinate, make refractory, affect with convulsion, (Lt) stimulate, intoxicate, (T) limp';
origin_explanation: the derivation from `azzaza `be strong', `as`azaza `sich versteifen', that is, `starr, steif sein' suggested by Stade 37 is unlikely.
